steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: “The gaseous ______ have molar masses of 42.08 and 44.10 g/mol, respectively.” The blank will contain “compounds” plus an appositive naming the two specific compounds. A restrictive appositive (essential to identifying which thing is meant) takes no commas. A non-restrictive appositive (removable extra info) takes commas on both sides.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "compounds, propylene and propane,": Commas on both sides mark the appositive as non-restrictive (removable). But removing it leaves “The gaseous compounds have molar masses…” — without the names, we don’t know which compounds. The appositive is essential, so commas are wrong. ✗
  • "compounds propylene and propane": No commas — restrictive treatment. “The gaseous compounds propylene and propane have molar masses of 42.08 and 44.10 g/mol, respectively.” Grammatically correct; the names specify exactly which compounds. ✓
  • "compounds propylene and propane.": A period mid-sentence leaves “have molar masses of 42.08 and 44.10 g/mol, respectively” as a fragment with no subject. ✗
  • "compounds, propylene and propane": A comma before but none after creates an unpaired insertion. “The gaseous compounds, propylene and propane have molar masses…” is structurally broken. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
